evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns mentioned in the objective.

The provider should provide clarity of what will happen with the product when their cloud service stops: When buying of a device, I think it should be made clear for the customer which functionality will remain working "indefinitely" and which parts are sold as part of a service that can stop working at any given time or might become subject to an additional charge to provide that service.
The provider must have a plan for continuance of the product.
They should either be able to provide a (paid) alternative when stopping the cloud service or should give the end user the option to host those services themself.

paragraphs.text: I think it's good to look at problematic parts of software like the dark patterns mentioned in the objective. However, one important thing that I miss in the objectives is the impact of cloud services on the workings of equipment or software; More and more devices and services rely on an internet connection in order to work as advertised. However when buying "smart" devices, I basically want to use them without any interference from the supplier. Some examples: - If I buy a thermostat to control the temperature in my house, then I want it to do that, even when the supplier shuts down a platform - If I buy a smart speaker to use as an alarm clock in the morning, then I think it should be able to still do that 3 years after buying it and not only in the first couple of years. If I look at what happens then I see the following articles (in dutch): - Nest thermostats are limited to a basic set of functionality (https://tweakers.net/nieuws/234358/google-stopt-verkoop-nest-thermostaten-in-europa.html). I think this is an example of a bare minimum that a company has to offer. - I bought a Sonos speaker in order to wake me up in the morning. This worked fine, until they released a new app that removed a lot of functionality and resulted in the speaker using my alarms that I had set before, which would wake me up too early on days where I would want a later alarm and not waking me up if I needed to be up early. Sonos acknowledged the problems with the new app, but basically said that people would have to deal with it (https://tweakers.net/nieuws/225600/sonos-ziet-af-van-terugbrengen-oude-appversie-na-tegenvallende-testresultaten.html). Based on the above, I think that some rules need to be made for cloud services to provide clarity of how a product will function in the future: - The provider should provide clarity of what will happen with the product when their cloud service stops: When buying of a device, I think it should be made clear for the customer which functionality will remain working "indefinitely" and which parts are sold as part of a service that can stop working at any given time or might become subject to an additional charge to provide that service. - The provider must have a plan for continuance of the product. They should either be able to provide a (paid) alternative when stopping the cloud service or should give the end user the option to host those services themself. - "Smart" devices are obviously capable of performing certain tasks offline, I think companies should allow users to control this without requiring any cloud service or specific app. Ideally this would be a simple web page running on the device or adhering to an industry standard API. My examples are mentioning smart devices, but I think the same should apply to software that is "bought" (as in a indefinite license to use the product). Requiring a good distinction between what can and cannot be used indefinitely gives a better understanding for consumers of what they can expect.
